narrative: As a white male applicant with a strong family background in the high-income bracket, your law school application stood out in some ways, including your solid LSAT score of 39.50, which is above the typical student's average of 36.70, and your plan to attend full-time like the vast majority of students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 43% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your academic record compared to others in the dataset of past bar takers. The most influential factor pulling against you was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, which strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar failure despite your other strengths. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile also at 1, far under the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about early inconsistencies in your performance relative to peers who passed the bar. On a brighter note, your above-average LSAT was the top positive influence, pushing the prediction toward success and showing your potential in a critical area that often predicts bar outcomes well. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 sits slightly below the 3.21 average, it played a lesser role here. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having standout test scores and socioeconomic stability, but those low early deciles made the model view you as riskier than most approved applicants, even as a full-time student. This isn't a judgment on your character or drive—it's the model's data-driven way of spotting patterns from thousands of similar cases. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also gather letters highlighting your LSAT strength and full-time commitment to balance the narrative. With targeted steps like these, reapplying next cycle could yield a very different outcome.
